title: Eastern and Western fronts of World War I
topic_path: Western and Eastern fronts of World War I
Eastern and Western fronts of World War I

assessment_items.question_content:
At what river in France were both Germany's initial drive in the Fall of 1914 and its Spring Offensive in 1918 halted?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In the Fall of 1914, Germany's offensive was stopped at the Battle of the Marne (First Battle of the Marne).  In Spring Offensive of 1918, they were also stopped at the Marne (Second Battle of the Marne).

It is covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-fighting/v/schlieffen-plan-and-the-first-battle-of-the-marne).

assessment_items.question_content:
This was a seminal battle in 1916 where the strategy of the Germans was to attack French positions from multiple sides and "bleed" the French army into capitulation (The idea being that since France couldn't afford to lose the position, they would throw in every man they had to defend it or capitulate).  Despite over 500,000 deaths on both sides, the French were able to successfully hold their position.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The First Battle of the Marne was in 1914 during the first German push into France.  The Second was in 1918 during the German Spring Offensive.
The Battle of the Somme was another seminal battle in 1916 with huge casualties.  It was mainly between British and German forces.
This description is for the Battle of Verdun.

assessment_items.question_content:
This Treaty in 1918 gave substantial Russian territory to the Central Powers but became void due to the Treaty of Versailles.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
By March of 1918, the Russians were essentially forced to sign the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk which conceded huge swaths of Russian territory to the Central Powers.

Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-fighting/v/world-war-i-eastern-front).

assessment_items.question_content:
Which is **NOT** true of the dynamics of World War I in early 1918 (January and February)?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Question covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-fighting/v/closing-stages-of-world-war-i).

Russia was not looking to capture territory. The Treaty of Brest-Litovsk in March 1918 actually gave huge chunks of the Russian Empire over to the Central Powers.

In late 1918, the Bolsheviks had taken control in Russia and were eager to extricate themselves from the War and quickly signed an armistice with the Central Powers.  This allowed Germany to begin redeploying troops to the Western Front (which had been a defensive stalemate for much of the War).  If they could go on the offensive before significant reinforcement from the United States arrived, they had a chance of victory.  On the other hand, it could go the other way if the American troops arrived in time.

choices.content: Russia was looking to capture as much territory as possible before an armistice was signed with the Central Powers.
The Allies were eager for American troops to arrive in significant numbers before Germany had a chance to redeploy troops from the Eastern Front
Germany was planning its Spring Offensive to knock-out the Allies on the Western Front before significant American reinforcement could arrive.
It was unclear whether the Allies or Central Powers would win the War.
Bolshevik Russia had taken itself out of the war and was in negotiations with the Central Powers over the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk.

assessment_items.question_content:
What type of naval vessel allowed the Germans to not be completely dominated by the British at sea during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/american-entry-world-war-I/v/blockades--u-boats-and-sinking-of-the-lusitania)

The British had the dominant surface fleet in the world entering into World War I.  Germany's surface fleet was mainly relegated to a corner of the North Sea during the war.  What allowed them to attempt to blockade the British was their use of submarines (U-boats).

assessment_items.question_content:
Russia signed an armistice with the Central Powers to end fighting on the Eastern Front in this December of this year (although hostilities resumed briefly the next year before a formal treaty was signed). 

**In what year did Russia sign this armistice?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
The Bolsheviks took power in Russia in November of 1917 and were eager to end fighting with the Central Powers and focus on consolidating control domestically.  Because of this, they signed an armistice with the Central Powers in December of 1917 (which was briefly broken in early 1918 as negotiations broke down and the Central Powers captured more territory), eventually leading to the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk in March of 1918.

This is covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-fighting/v/world-war-i-eastern-front) .

assessment_items.question_content:
The armistice on the Western Front of World War I was signed on which date.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Armistice Day, which marked the end of fighting on the Western Front (and the end of World War I) was on the 11th hour (11am) of the 11th day of the 11th month (November 11, 1918).

This is covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-fighting/v/closing-stages-of-world-war-i).

assessment_items.question_content:
Which best describes fighting on the Eastern and Western Fronts during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Covered in [this video comparing the Eastern and Western Fronts](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-fighting/v/comparing-the-eastern-and-western-fronts-in-wwi).

The Western Front was short enough (essentially the northwest corner of France and a bit of Belgium) to make trench warfare feasible.
The Eastern Front, however, was much longer (more than a thousand miles) which could never be practically defended with trenches.
Trench warfare favored the defense.  Because of this, the Western Front devolved into a defensive stalemate while the Eastern Front was much more dynamic and fluid.

choices.content: They were both characterized by trench warfare that gave the advantage to the defense
The Western Front was a defensive stalemate from 1914-1918 while the Eastern Front was much more fluid
The Western Front was much longer than the Eastern Front which made trench warfare impractical
The technology used in the Eastern Front was more advanced than that used in the Western Front
The Western Front was won quickly which allowed for a longer, protracted conflict on the Eastern Front

assessment_items.question_content:
What best describes the Schlieffen Plan?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/world-war-I-fighting/v/schlieffen-plan-and-the-first-battle-of-the-marne).

The Schlieffen Plan was Germany's way of dealing with a two front war against the French and the Russians.  Russia had a huge army which would take time to mobilize.  Under this plan, the Germans would use that time to execute a quick and massive invasion to knock out the French (on the Western Front) so that they could then focus on the Russians (on the Eastern Front).

choices.content: German plan to use unrestricted submarine warfare to block the United Kingdom
German plan to quickly knock out the French on the Western Front, then a single-front war with the Russians
German plan to help the Austrians invade Serbia
